paragraph: 12. The policy adopted regarding the language of literacy should be rigorously defined in multilingual contexts, particularly when the national or official language differs from local languages. The use of the mother tongue is desirable; however, in some cases, it will be appropriate to encourage bilingual literacy.

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph addresses language policy in education, which directly relates to curriculum and pedagogy policies. It emphasizes multilingual contexts and mother tongue use, aligning with inclusion and diversity. 'Governance and planning' is excluded as the focus is on instructional practices, not policy frameworks.
low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph discusses defining language policies in multilingual contexts (Design and development) and implementing literacy strategies (Curriculum implementation). 'Content and learning areas' is excluded as it focuses on subject matter, not language policy. 'Curriculum evaluation and reform' is not mentioned. 'Competency-based' and 'Other' are irrelevant. 'Organization and instructional time' is not addressed.
low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph focuses on language policy in multilingual contexts and literacy instruction, aligning with 'General (academic)' as it addresses educational orientation and curriculum design. 'Vocational/technical (TVET)' and 'Combined general and vocational' are excluded as the text does not mention vocational skills or combined approaches.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 13. A needs assessment procedure should be followed to determine the functional objectives and the content of literacy and education for children and adults. Basic education programs should be designed as means to meet the fundamental educational needs of all and to strengthen learners' motivation by directly addressing the concerns of their daily lives.

low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph discusses designing basic education programs to meet fundamental needs and addresses content related to daily life concerns, aligning with 'Design and development' and 'Content and learning areas'. 'Curriculum implementation' and 'evaluation' are not explicitly mentioned. 'Competency-based' is not directly referenced. 'Other' is avoided due to specificity.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes needs assessment procedures and designing education programs to meet fundamental needs, aligning with pedagogical foundations. It also mentions assessment as part of determining objectives, supporting 'Assessment and feedback'. 'Teaching methods' is not explicitly discussed, and other categories like technology or professional learning communities are not mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph explicitly mentions 'children and adults' in the context of literacy and education, directly supporting both categories. 'Children (0-17)' is explicitly named, while 'Adults and lifelong learners' aligns with 'basic education programs' aimed at all learners. Other categories like 'low-income' or 'disadvantaged' are not explicitly mentioned, and the focus is on age groups rather than socioeconomic or conflict-related contexts.
low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph emphasizes literacy and basic education programs aimed at meeting fundamental educational needs and addressing daily life concerns, aligning with general academic orientation. Vocational/technical categories are excluded as the focus is on foundational education rather than job-specific skills. Combined categories are not selected due to lack of explicit vocational references.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 14. It is appropriate to involve and engage potential young and adult participants in literacy programs in the identification and structuring of their fundamental educational needs, so that learning becomes more meaningful to them and they themselves become more strongly motivated.

low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph explicitly mentions 'young and adult participants' in literacy programs, directly supporting both 'Youth and adolescents' and 'Adults and lifelong learners'. Other categories like 'Children (0-17)' are excluded as the focus is on participants rather than children specifically. 'Learners with disabilities' and 'Indigenous or minority groups' are not mentioned, so they are omitted.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes structuring educational needs and meaningful learning, aligning with pedagogical foundations. It also mentions motivation and structured learning, which relate to assessment and feedback mechanisms. 'Teaching methods' and 'learner population' are less fitting as the focus is on learning design rather than specific methods or demographics.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 15. To increase the relevance of program content, its definition should take into account not only the needs of the learners but also various factors inherent to the environment and context. These factors may be professional or geographical in nature, relate to issues of the respective status of men and women, or be cultural, technological, or otherwise.

low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes contextual factors like environment, culture, gender, and professional/geographical aspects, which align with 'Other cross-cutting theme' as these factors are not fully captured by existing categories. Categories like 'Human rights and equality' (gender issues) and 'Sustainable development and climate action' (environment) are partially relevant but insufficient. 'Citizenship and civic education' and 'Global citizenship and peace education' are too narrow. The orchestrator summary supports 'Cross-cutting themes & skills' as a broader category, justifying 'Other cross-cutting theme' as the most fitting.
low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph emphasizes designing programs considering environmental, cultural, and contextual factors, aligning with 'Design and development'. It also mentions content relevance and learner needs, fitting 'Content and learning areas'. 'Curriculum implementation' and 'evaluation' are excluded as the text focuses on design rather than delivery or assessment. 'Competency-based' is not explicitly mentioned, and 'other' is avoided due to specificity.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph emphasizes factors like gender, geography, and socio-economic status, aligning with 'All learners' as a broad category. 'Women and girls' is explicitly mentioned in the context of gender status. 'Rural and remote learners' and 'Low-income and disadvantaged groups' are directly referenced as contextual factors affecting learners.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: As a component of continuing literacy, post-literacy, functional literacy, and continuing education programs should facilitate the continuation of learning in various fields, in accordance with needs assessments, by building on the knowledge already acquired to continue learning and by applying the acquired skills in diverse situations in support of personal, social, and economic development.

low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph emphasizes designing programs that build on existing knowledge (Design and development), specifies learning fields and application contexts (Content and learning areas), and discusses implementation through needs assessments and skill application (Curriculum implementation). 'Curriculum evaluation and reform' is excluded as there's no mention of assessment or reform. 'Competency-based' is not explicitly framed. 'Other' is avoided due to specificity.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes building on prior knowledge and applying skills for development, aligning with pedagogical foundations. 'Assessment and feedback' is included due to the focus on needs assessments and skill application. 'Teaching methods' and 'technology' are not explicitly mentioned, while 'professional communities' and 'other' are less directly supported.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes applying skills for personal, social, and economic development, aligning with 'Life skills and 21st century competencies'. 'Sustainable development and climate action' is supported by 'social and economic development' references. 'Inclusive education and special needs' fits through 'needs assessments' and building on prior knowledge. Other categories like digital literacy or human rights are not explicitly mentioned.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 17. Teaching and learning methods should be developed, educational materials designed, and technologies and media used that are adapted to the pace and mode of learning for adults on the one hand, and children on the other. The quality of textbooks and other teaching and learning tools should be continuously improved through the evaluation of the education system as a whole.

low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph explicitly distinguishes between adults and children as separate learner groups, directly supporting both categories. Other categories like 'All learners' are too broad and not specifically mentioned. The focus on adapting methods to different age groups justifies these two sub-labels.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes adapting methods and materials to different learner populations (adults/children), aligning with 'Teaching methods and approaches'. It mentions improving quality through system evaluation, linking to 'Assessment and feedback'. 'Pedagogical foundations and theories' is supported by the focus on developing methods and adapting to learning modes. 'Technology-supported pedagogy' is excluded as technology isn't explicitly mentioned. 'Professional learning communities' and 'Other pedagogy related issue' lack direct evidence.
low_level_thinking.Education system monitoring & evaluation: The paragraph emphasizes continuous improvement through system evaluation, aligning with 'Quality assurance and accreditation' and 'Institutional evaluation and inspection'. 'Learning assessments and examinations' is excluded as the focus is on systemic evaluation, not specific assessments. 'EMIS and data systems' is not explicitly mentioned, and 'Research and surveys' lacks direct reference.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: Educational technologies, including new technologies, should serve to complement the teacher’s work. New educational technologies, when available—such as microcomputers, television programs, videocassettes, and audiocassettes, etc., specially designed—could be used judiciously for teaching and to enhance motivation by discussing them to analyze and interpret. Similarly, new information and communication technologies could be employed, where appropriate, for distance learning, in order to benefit those living in rural or remote regions with literacy and education programs.

low_level_thinking.Learning modality: The paragraph discusses technologies complementing teaching, which aligns with formal education. Mention of distance learning and rural populations suggests non-formal education. Informal learning is implied through self-directed use of media. Lifelong learning is excluded as the focus is on specific technologies rather than continuous learning across life stages.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph explicitly discusses integrating educational technologies (microcomputers, ICT) into teaching methods to enhance motivation and support distance learning, aligning with 'Technology-supported pedagogy'. It also describes using these tools to 'teach and enhance motivation' and 'benefit those in rural areas', which reflects 'Teaching methods and approaches'. 'Pedagogical foundations' and 'assessment' are not directly addressed, while 'professional learning communities' and 'curriculum' are not mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Infrastructure & resources: The paragraph explicitly mentions educational technologies like microcomputers, television programs, and information and communication technologies, aligning with 'ICT and digital infrastructure'. It also references materials such as videocassettes and audiocassettes as teaching tools, fitting 'Teaching and learning materials'. Other categories like school buildings or WASH facilities are not mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph explicitly mentions benefiting those in rural or remote regions, directly supporting 'Rural and remote learners'. It also implies accessibility challenges by referencing technologies 'when available', suggesting 'Low-income and disadvantaged groups' face resource barriers. Other categories like 'Children' or 'Women' are not specifically mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Teachers: The paragraph focuses on educational technologies complementing teachers' work and enhancing teaching methods, aligning with 'Teacher other' which covers broader roles and integration of tools. Other categories like 'Professional development' or 'Working conditions' are not explicitly mentioned. 'Curriculum' and 'Pedagogy & assessment' are implied but not directly labeled under 'Teachers' subcategories here.
low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph focuses on educational technologies enhancing teaching methods and learning experiences, aligning with 'General (academic)'. 'Vocational/technical (TVET)' is excluded as the text does not mention vocational skills. 'Combined general and vocational' is rejected since the emphasis is on general education technologies rather than dual-purpose applications.
low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph discusses integrating new technologies into teaching practices, aligning with 'Curriculum implementation' through instructional methods. 'Other curriculum-related issue' covers broader educational technology use. 'Design and development' is excluded as it focuses on creation, not application. 'Content and learning areas' is not directly addressed.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 19. Teaching and learning procedures should be made more dynamic by employing methods such as dialogue and mutual teaching, active learning in small groups, and self-instruction. All of these methods are likely to increase the motivation, autonomy, and self-confidence of the learner as well as the capacities of the teacher. The atmosphere of adult literacy classes should be made more lively and attractive, incorporating creative and local cultural activities that encourage learners to continue attending these classes.

low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph explicitly mentions dynamic teaching methods like dialogue, mutual teaching, and active learning, supporting 'Teaching methods and approaches'. It also emphasizes learner-centered, autonomy-focused pedagogy, aligning with 'Pedagogical foundations and theories'. 'Assessment and feedback' is excluded as no evaluation methods are discussed. 'Technology-supported pedagogy' is not mentioned. 'Professional learning communities' and 'Other pedagogy related issue' lack direct evidence.
low_level_thinking.Learning modality: The paragraph emphasizes learner-centered, flexible methods like dialogue, small group activities, and cultural engagement, aligning with non-formal education's focus on adult learners and community-based approaches. Informal learning is supported by the emphasis on self-instruction and creative activities that encourage continued participation. Formal education and lifelong learning are less directly addressed, as the text does not mention structured curricula or broader lifelong learning frameworks.
low_level_thinking.Teachers: The paragraph emphasizes dynamic teaching methods and learner-centered approaches, aligning with professional development for teachers. 'Teacher other' is included due to mentions of classroom atmosphere and cultural activities affecting teaching practices. Categories like recruitment, salaries, or legal status are not addressed.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph specifically mentions 'adult literacy classes' directly linking to 'Adults and lifelong learners'. The emphasis on increasing motivation and self-confidence suggests addressing disadvantaged groups. Other categories like children or youth are excluded as the focus is on adult education. 'Low-income' is implied through the context of literacy programs often targeting disadvantaged populations.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes dynamic teaching methods like dialogue, active learning, and self-instruction, aligning with 'Life skills and 21st century competencies' through learner autonomy and motivation. 'Inclusive education and special needs' is supported by references to cultural activities that make classes accessible and engaging for diverse learners. Other categories like digital literacy or civic education are not explicitly mentioned.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 20. A robust infrastructure should be established to provide flexible and dynamic modes of operation for non-formal adult literacy, post-literacy, and functional literacy programs, as well as for non-formal primary education aimed at out-of-school children. The development of non-formal education, an approach that is beginning to emerge in some countries, should be considered within the framework of adult education structures in collaboration with enterprises, social services, agricultural extension units, and so forth.

low_level_thinking.Learning modality: The paragraph explicitly mentions non-formal education programs and structures, directly supporting 'Non-formal education'. 'Lifelong learning' is implied through references to adult literacy, post-literacy, and functional literacy, which align with continuous learning across life stages. 'Formal education' is excluded as the focus is on non-formal alternatives. 'Informal learning' is not explicitly mentioned, and 'Infrastructure & resources' is a separate debate theme per the summary.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph mentions collaboration with enterprises, social services, and agricultural extension units, indicating involvement of the private sector and industry. It also refers to adult education structures, implying government and policy makers' role. Parents and communities are implied through the mention of social services and agricultural extension units, which often engage local communities.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph explicitly mentions 'out-of-school children' for non-formal primary education, supporting 'Children (0-17)'. 'Adults and lifelong learners' is implied through references to adult literacy, post-literacy, and functional literacy programs. Other categories like 'Youth and adolescents' are not specifically mentioned, and 'All learners' is too broad without direct evidence.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes non-formal adult literacy and education structures, aligning with 'Lifelong learning and adult education'. Governance is addressed through collaboration with multiple stakeholders. International cooperation is implied by collaboration with enterprises and social services.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 21. For adult literacy and continuing education, assessment should be a positive process aimed at making the individual aware of their progress as well as helping them identify their shortcomings. It should be an integral part of all important aspects of literacy programs in order to contribute to their improvement. An appropriate cumulative evaluation mechanism should be developed and implemented with this objective in mind. Efforts should be made to award a certificate to participants who have successfully completed a literacy course.

low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes assessment as a positive, progress-focused process and its integration into literacy programs, aligning with 'Assessment and feedback'. It also discusses pedagogical approaches to learning and improvement, supporting 'Pedagogical foundations and theories'. 'Education system monitoring & evaluation' was excluded as the focus is on individual assessment rather than systemic evaluation.
low_level_thinking.Education system monitoring & evaluation: The paragraph emphasizes assessment processes for adult literacy and continuing education, aligning with 'Learning assessments and examinations'. The mention of developing and implementing an evaluation mechanism supports 'Quality assurance and accreditation'. 'Education statistics and indicators' is excluded as the focus is on assessment processes, not data collection. 'EMIS and data systems' is not directly mentioned. 'Institutional evaluation and inspection' and 'Research and surveys' are less relevant here.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes assessment processes and evaluation mechanisms to improve literacy programs, aligning with 'Quality and learning outcomes'. It also mentions adult literacy and continuing education, directly linking to 'Lifelong learning and adult education'. 'Curriculum' and 'Education system monitoring' were excluded as per the summary, while 'Policy theme' was considered too broad.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 22. Evaluation should generally include: (a) an assessment of the learner’s outcomes; (b) an evaluation of the teaching program; (c) monitoring of program implementation to optimize its internal and external effectiveness; (d) from time to time, a comprehensive review of the program to renovate and strengthen its objectives, organization and management, communication channels, effectiveness of structures, etc.; (e) a cost evaluation; (f) an impact assessment to understand and enhance the effects of literacy on the learner’s life, their family, the community to which they belong, and society as a whole.

low_level_thinking.Education system monitoring & evaluation: The paragraph emphasizes program implementation monitoring, comprehensive reviews of objectives/management, and impact assessments, aligning with 'Institutional evaluation and inspection'. Cost evaluation and effectiveness optimization relate to 'Quality assurance and accreditation'. Impact assessments are excluded from 'Cross-cutting themes' as they are framed as part of institutional review. 'Learning assessments' and 'EMIS' are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph explicitly mentions evaluation, assessment of learner outcomes, impact assessment, and program monitoring, directly aligning with 'Assessment and feedback'. 'Other pedagogy related issue' captures broader systemic evaluation and optimization aspects not fully covered by other categories. 'Pedagogical foundations and theories' and 'Teaching methods and approaches' are excluded as the text focuses on evaluation processes rather than theoretical frameworks or specific teaching techniques.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes evaluation processes, program optimization, and impact assessment, which align with 'Life skills and 21st century competencies' through systemic oversight and effectiveness. 'Other cross-cutting theme' captures broader societal impacts like community and societal effects mentioned. Categories like 'Digital literacy' or 'Human rights' lack direct textual support. 'Policy theme' and 'Stakeholder focus' are debated but not explicitly labeled here.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes evaluation of learner outcomes (Quality and learning outcomes), program monitoring and optimization (Governance and planning), and impact assessment (Accountability and transparency). 'Pedagogy & assessment' is excluded as it focuses on methodological evaluation rather than systemic oversight. 'Cross-cutting themes' is omitted due to lack of explicit societal impact language. 'Policy theme' is not selected as it lacks governance-specific language.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes impact assessment on learners' lives, families, and communities, directly linking to learners and students. Parents and communities are explicitly mentioned as recipients of literacy effects. Civil society and NGOs are implied through references to societal impacts and program optimization, which often involve non-state actors.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 23. A monitoring plan for the progress of the program should be established, particularly during its initial phases, as the implementation stage is the most critical and it is at this time that obstacles, problems, and unforeseen blockages multiply, which can distort the final outcome even if the literacy campaign has been well prepared. By periodically observing how educational, administrative, and financial functions are performed at the national, intermediate, and local levels, a robust monitoring system should identify implementation shortcomings, thereby allowing timely corrective measures to optimize the program’s results and thus ensure its success.

low_level_thinking.Education system monitoring & evaluation: The paragraph emphasizes monitoring implementation across national, intermediate, and local levels, aligning with 'Institutional evaluation and inspection'. It also mentions identifying shortcomings and corrective measures to optimize results, supporting 'Quality assurance and accreditation'. 'Education statistics and indicators' is excluded as the focus is on monitoring processes rather than data collection. Other categories like EMIS or learning assessments are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes establishing a monitoring plan and periodic observation of implementation across levels, aligning with governance and planning. It also highlights identifying shortcomings and corrective measures, supporting accountability and transparency. Other categories like quality or financing are not directly addressed.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 24. The evaluation of the impact of literacy and basic education at both individual and collective levels must demonstrate how, in given contexts or conditions, education and training contribute to improving health and hygiene, increasing life expectancy, reducing infant mortality, enhancing participation in vaccination campaigns, improving childcare, raising family income, boosting self-confidence and autonomy, intensifying social, economic, and cultural participation, and enabling the full exercise of civic rights and duties. It is essential to understand the factors that produce these effects in order to achieve national development objectives.

low_level_thinking.Education system monitoring & evaluation: The paragraph emphasizes evaluating impacts through factors affecting national development, aligning with 'Research and surveys' for analytical studies. 'Education statistics and indicators' fits due to references to measurable outcomes like health, income, and civic rights. Other categories like 'EMIS and data systems' or 'Institutional evaluation' are less directly supported by the focus on impact analysis and development goals.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes evaluation of education's impact on health, hygiene, life expectancy, and development objectives, aligning with Health and well-being policies and Sustainable development and environment. Quality and learning outcomes is included as it addresses the effectiveness of education in achieving these impacts. Categories like Governance and planning or Financing are excluded as the focus is on outcomes, not implementation mechanisms.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph explicitly mentions health and hygiene, vaccination campaigns, and life expectancy, aligning with 'Health and well-being education'. 'Human rights and equality' is supported by references to civic rights and duties. 'Life skills and 21st century competencies' fits self-confidence, autonomy, and social participation. Other categories like digital literacy or economic literacy are not directly mentioned, and 'inclusive education' lacks explicit reference.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 25. The training of teachers, both male and female, is one of the main components of quality primary education. In certain Member States, it would be beneficial for this training to include adult education in order to enable teachers, if necessary, to carry out some teaching tasks within this framework. Innovative measures for recruitment and specific training should be implemented, allowing professional mobility for motivated teachers and the recognition of skills acquired in the field by literacy practitioners.

low_level_thinking.Teachers: The paragraph emphasizes teacher training (Initial education and training) and professional mobility/skill recognition (Professional development). Recruitment and mobility are linked to addressing shortages (Teacher supply and shortages). Legal status, working conditions, and evaluation are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes teacher training and professional mobility, aligning with 'Teachers and workforce policies'. Mention of adult education and skill recognition ties to 'Lifelong learning and adult education'. 'Innovation and technology policies' covers innovative recruitment and training measures. 'Curriculum' and 'pedagogy' are excluded as the focus is on workforce and adult education, not curriculum content.
low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph emphasizes teacher training design (Design and development), implementation of innovative measures (Curriculum implementation), and recognition of skills (Competency-based and learning outcomes). 'Content and learning areas' is excluded as the focus is on training methods, not content. 'Organization and instructional time' is not explicitly mentioned. 'Curriculum evaluation and reform' is not directly addressed.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes professional mobility and skill recognition, aligning with 'Life skills and 21st century competencies' through adaptability and skill development. 'Inclusive education and special needs' is included due to mention of adult education and literacy practitioners. 'Other cross-cutting theme' covers professional mobility and recognition of field-acquired skills not fully captured by other categories.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes teacher training components (Pedagogical foundations), innovative recruitment/training methods (Teaching methods), and professional mobility/skill recognition (Professional learning communities). 'Assessment and feedback' is excluded as no evaluation mechanisms are mentioned. 'Technology-supported pedagogy' is omitted due to lack of tech references. 'Other pedagogy related issue' is avoided to maintain specificity.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes teacher training, professional mobility, and skill recognition, directly aligning with 'Teachers and educators'. 'Government and policy makers' is included as the text discusses policy-related aspects like training implementation and curriculum integration. 'Learners and students' is excluded as the focus is on educators, not learners. 'Civil society' and 'private sector' are not mentioned, and 'infrastructure' is not addressed.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 26. The variety of tasks assigned to literacy personnel requires specific skills. Literacy workers should therefore receive solid specialized training, including theoretical presentations, reflection, and practice in adult pedagogy. The same considerations should apply to those responsible for management, inspection, program development, or other pedagogical functions.

low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes specialized training in adult pedagogy, theoretical presentations, and practice, aligning with 'Pedagogical foundations and theories' and 'Teaching methods and approaches'. 'Professional learning communities' is included due to the mention of training and development for literacy workers. 'Assessment and feedback' is excluded as the focus is on training rather than evaluation. 'Technology-supported pedagogy' and 'Curriculum' are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes specialized training in adult pedagogy, which aligns with 'Life skills and 21st century competencies' through its focus on practical and theoretical skills. 'Inclusive education and special needs' is relevant due to the mention of diverse roles (management, inspection, program development) requiring tailored training. Other categories like 'Digital and ICT literacy' or 'Entrepreneurship' are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Teachers: The paragraph emphasizes specialized training and practice in adult pedagogy, aligning with 'Initial education and training'. It also mentions ongoing considerations for management and pedagogical roles, supporting 'Professional development'. 'Recruitment' and 'qualification' are not explicitly addressed, and other categories lack direct evidence.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 27. To maintain the quality and effectiveness of the entire program, continuous training for all personnel must be organized. Furthermore, to promote self-learning and continuing education among literacy workers, as well as trainers and other literacy staff, distance education programs, journals, bulletins, and other similar resources should be made available to them. Higher education institutions, faculties of education, and regional training centers that collaborate with UNESCO, such as CREFAL and ASFEC, as well as national training centers, must participate in the initial and ongoing training of teachers and other officials working in the field of literacy.

low_level_thinking.Learning modality: The paragraph emphasizes continuous training and self-learning, aligning with 'Lifelong learning'. Distance education and resources like journals support 'Non-formal education'. 'Formal education' is excluded as it refers to structured institutions, while 'Informal learning' is less explicitly supported.
low_level_thinking.Infrastructure & resources: The paragraph mentions distance education programs, journals, and bulletins, which fall under teaching and learning materials. Distance education implies ICT infrastructure. Other categories like classrooms or physical infrastructure are not mentioned. WASH, transport, and libraries are irrelevant here.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes training for teachers and literacy staff, directly linking to 'Teachers and educators'. UNESCO and its partners like CREFAL and ASFEC are mentioned, fitting 'International organizations and donors'. 'Other' is included due to references to distance education resources and journals, which don't neatly fit existing categories. 'Government and policy makers' is excluded as no direct mention of governments. 'Civil society' and 'private sector' are not referenced.
low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph emphasizes continuous training and resources like journals/bulletins, aligning with general academic development. Vocational/technical is excluded as there's no mention of job-specific skills. Combined is rejected due to lack of clear vocational elements.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes continuous training and quality maintenance (Quality and learning outcomes), teacher training (Teachers and workforce policies), distance education technologies (Innovation and technology policies), and collaboration with UNESCO partners (International cooperation and partnerships). 'Lifelong learning' was excluded as the focus is on professional development rather than adult education.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 28. Public authorities must recognize that teachers play an essential role in all educational activities and, in accordance with Article 7 of the World Declaration on Education for All and Paragraph 33 of the Framework for Action to Meet Basic Learning Needs, as well as the joint ILO/UNESCO Recommendation concerning the Status of Teachers, should actively address their social status, living conditions, and remuneration, so that they can devote themselves fully to education and thereby avoid the wastage of their potential that would result if they were forced to seek other means of livelihood.

low_level_thinking.Teachers: The paragraph explicitly mentions addressing teachers' social status, living conditions, and remuneration, directly linking to 'Working conditions and salaries'. It also references international declarations and recommendations, supporting 'Legal status and rights'. Categories like 'Recruitment and hiring' or 'Professional development' are not explicitly mentioned, and 'Teacher supply and shortages' is not addressed.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph directly addresses teachers' social status, living conditions, and remuneration, aligning with 'Teachers and workforce policies'. It also mentions policy implementation through active addressing of these issues, fitting 'Governance and planning'. 'Legal frameworks' is excluded as the focus is on policy actions rather than new legal instruments.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph explicitly mentions teachers and their social status, remuneration, and living conditions, directly aligning with 'Teachers and educators'. 'Government and policy makers' are referenced through the obligation on public authorities to address these issues. Other categories like 'Legal frameworks' are excluded as the focus is on implementation rather than new legal instruments.
low_level_thinking.Legal frameworks: The paragraph references existing international instruments (World Declaration, Framework for Action, ILO/UNESCO Recommendation) which fall under 'Education laws and regulations'. It also outlines obligations for public authorities regarding teachers' status, remuneration, and conditions, aligning with 'Rights and obligations of stakeholders'. 'Legal frameworks' is excluded as the text focuses on applying existing norms rather than creating new legal instruments.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 29. Awareness campaigns and the services currently provided in both industrialized and developing countries should be significantly expanded in order to meet the basic educational needs of all adults. A systematic study of the issue based on research should aim to identify groups and individuals with insufficient schooling levels, including migrant workers and their families, while respecting their linguistic and cultural identity, to determine the quantitative significance of these groups and to establish reliable statistical data. It would also be appropriate to define the nature and extent of the basic educational needs of these various groups and individuals, taking into account cumulative factors such as unemployment, poverty, social marginalization, etc. With changes in employment structure, demographic evolution, and technological progress, these needs become recurrent, generating a permanent demand for fundamental learning in the form of flexible and sustainable educational services, calling for a long-term commitment from public authorities in both industrialized and developing countries.

low_level_thinking.Education level: The paragraph discusses expanding education for all adults, including marginalized groups like migrant workers, and addresses varying needs across different factors. It references both basic educational needs and long-term commitment, indicating multiple levels. Other categories like primary or secondary are excluded as the focus is on adult education and lifelong learning, not specific grade levels.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph explicitly mentions expanding education for adults, including migrant workers and those facing unemployment/poverty, aligning with 'Adults and lifelong learners' and 'Migrants, refugees and displaced persons'. 'Low-income and disadvantaged groups' is supported by references to poverty and social marginalization. Other categories like 'Children' or 'Youth' are excluded as the focus is on adults. 'Indigenous' and 'Disabilities' are not mentioned, and 'Conflict settings' are not referenced.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes expanding adult education (Lifelong learning and adult education) and addressing marginalized groups like migrant workers (Inclusion and diversity). Governance and planning is highlighted through calls for systematic studies, statistical data, and long-term public commitment. Other categories like financing or curriculum are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes expanding education for marginalized groups like migrant workers, aligning with 'Inclusive education and special needs'. It also mentions lifelong learning and flexible services due to changing societal factors, fitting 'Life skills and 21st century competencies'. 'Other cross-cutting theme' was excluded as specific categories better capture the focus on marginalized populations and adaptive learning needs.
low_level_thinking.Learning modality: The paragraph emphasizes expanding educational services for adults and addressing recurrent learning needs through flexible, sustainable services, aligning with 'Non-formal education' and 'Lifelong learning'. 'Formal education' is excluded as the focus is on adult education beyond traditional systems. 'Informal learning' is less directly supported by the normative language on structured services.
low_level_thinking.Education system monitoring & evaluation: The paragraph emphasizes systematic research to identify marginalized groups and collect statistical data, aligning with 'Research and surveys' and 'Education statistics and indicators'. 'Learning assessments' and 'EMIS' are excluded as the focus is on data collection rather than standardized testing or management systems. 'Quality assurance' and 'institutional evaluation' are not mentioned, and 'other' is avoided due to specificity.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes expanding education for adults (learners/students) and calls for systematic research and long-term public commitment (government/policy makers). Marginalized groups like migrant workers are identified as learners, while policy implementation and statistical data collection align with government responsibility.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 30. In both industrialized and developing countries, the public sector should sustainably play a central role in financing adult literacy. This literacy should continue to diversify to meet the specific needs of individuals and groups. Measures should be taken to establish a pedagogical partnership with the school education system, including post-secondary institutions and workplace training programs as well as programs focused on community needs. Furthermore, additional measures could be implemented to establish an institutional partnership involving multiple ministries (education, labor, agriculture, etc.), as well as businesses, local authorities, communities, the volunteer sector, and other non-governmental institutions. Where they do not already exist, appropriate infrastructures should be established at the national, regional, and local levels to guide research, develop services both quantitatively and qualitatively, and ensure that suitable systems for concrete field action receive the necessary support.

low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph emphasizes literacy as a foundational educational goal (General (academic)) and mentions workplace training and community needs (Combined general and vocational). Vocational elements are present but not dominant, so 'Vocational/technical (TVET)' is excluded.
low_level_thinking.Ownership/Provision: The paragraph emphasizes public sector leadership ('public sector should sustainably play a central role'), multi-stakeholder partnerships (involving ministries, businesses, communities, NGOs), and community-focused programs. 'Public-private partnership' aligns with institutional collaborations. 'Community or faith-based' matches community involvement. 'Private non-profit/for-profit' are excluded as no private entities are explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Infrastructure & resources: The paragraph emphasizes establishing infrastructures at multiple levels to guide research, develop services, and support field action. None of the specific categories (school buildings, ICT, WASH, etc.) directly match this broad infrastructure support role. 'Other' is the only category that can encompass the systemic infrastructure development mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes public sector leadership (Government and policy makers), community needs (Parents and communities), partnerships with NGOs and civil society (Civil society and NGOs), and collaboration with businesses (Private sector and industry). Multi-stakeholder partnerships are explicitly mentioned, excluding 'Education orientation' and 'Infrastructure & resources' as primary categories.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes adult literacy as part of lifelong learning, mentions multi-ministry and multi-stakeholder partnerships (international cooperation), and discusses institutional frameworks and infrastructure development (governance). 'Financing and resource mobilization' is implied but not explicitly stated. 'Curriculum and pedagogy policies' is not directly addressed.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes pedagogical partnerships with education systems and institutional collaboration, aligning with 'Pedagogical foundations and theories'. It also mentions multi-ministry and stakeholder partnerships, fitting 'Professional learning communities'. 'Teaching methods' and 'assessment' are not explicitly addressed, and 'technology' is absent.
low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph emphasizes establishing partnerships and infrastructures for curriculum delivery (Curriculum implementation), coordinating multiple stakeholders and sectors (Organization and instructional time), and mentions systemic integration beyond traditional education frameworks (Other curriculum-related issue). Content and learning areas are not explicitly addressed, while competency-based outcomes and evaluation are not directly mentioned.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 31. In all countries, efforts should be made to invest more in research to determine the best ways to meet the requirements for raising the fundamental skill levels of the population. Universities and other institutions should contribute to this field.

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes improving fundamental skills (Quality and learning outcomes) through research investment. Governance and planning are addressed via institutional collaboration. Financing and resource mobilization are implied by calls for increased investment. Other categories like 'Innovation and technology policies' are excluded as the focus is on research rather than tech-specific policies.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes investment in research and institutional collaboration, aligning with government policy roles in resource allocation. Universities are explicitly mentioned as key contributors, justifying their inclusion. Other categories like educators or communities are not directly referenced.
low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph emphasizes research investment and institutional collaboration (universities and other institutions), which aligns with general academic priorities. Vocational/technical (TVET) is excluded as there's no mention of job-specific skills or technical training. Combined general and vocational is rejected due to lack of explicit vocational focus.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 32. Industrialized and developing countries should engage in exchanges of experiences concerning common issues they face at the stage of developing programs tailored to meet the needs of their populations: issues related to the language of instruction, the dispersion of populations, the existence of areas of extreme poverty, the diversity of groups to be literate, and so forth.

Learner population.category: Population in conflict and emergencies settings

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes collaborative program development (Governance and planning), addresses diverse literacy needs and poverty (Inclusion and diversity), and mentions tailored programs related to curriculum elements like language of instruction (Curriculum and pedagogy policies). 'International cooperation' is excluded as the focus is on domestic program development rather than cross-border partnerships.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes collaboration between industrialized and developing countries (implying government involvement), communities facing poverty and dispersion (parents and communities), and populations needing literacy (learners). 'Teachers and educators' are not explicitly mentioned. 'Civil society' and 'private sector' are not directly referenced. 'Other' is avoided due to specificity.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph mentions 'diversity of groups to be literate' and 'areas of extreme poverty' supporting 'Low-income and disadvantaged groups' and 'Indigenous or minority groups'. 'Population in conflict and emergencies settings' is implied by 'extreme poverty' and 'dispersion of populations'. 'All learners' is broad enough to encompass these specific groups.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes diversity of groups to be literate and extreme poverty, aligning with 'Inclusive education and special needs'. 'Other cross-cutting theme' captures issues like language of instruction and dispersion, which don't fit other categories. 'Citizenship' and 'Human rights' are not explicitly mentioned, while 'Digital literacy' and others are irrelevant.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 33. Considering a global decade of action in favor of literacy, the UNESCO General Conference, at its twenty-fourth session (1987), called upon Member States, intergovernmental organizations, and non-governmental organizations to take immediate measures to prepare programs and activities for the International Literacy Year in the context of the action plan to eradicate illiteracy by the year 2000. In view of this appeal and other international and national developments, it is recommended that UNESCO, together with its Member States, undertake new concerted measures to eliminate illiteracy and ensure education for all during the current decade. UNESCO, in close cooperation with Member States and other United Nations agencies, should implement measures throughout the decade aimed at achieving basic education for all to eradicate illiteracy and universalize education.

low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph focuses on eradication of illiteracy and universal education, aligning with 'General (academic)' as it addresses foundational literacy and education for all. 'Combined general and vocational' was excluded as there is no mention of vocational/technical skills. 'Vocational/technical (TVET)' is not supported by the text.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes eradication of illiteracy and education for all, aligning with 'Access and equity'. Multi-stakeholder collaboration (UNESCO, Member States, UN agencies) supports 'International cooperation and partnerships'. 'Lifelong learning and adult education' is implied through the focus on basic education and universalizing education. 'Governance and planning' and 'Financing' are excluded as implementation details are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes collaboration between UNESCO (international organization), Member States (government), and NGOs/non-governmental organizations (civil society). 'Learners and students' is excluded as the focus is on systemic action rather than direct learner targeting. 'Private sector' and 'Parents and communities' are not mentioned, so they are omitted.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph emphasizes 'education for all' and 'eradicate illiteracy', aligning with 'All learners'. 'Low-income and disadvantaged groups' is supported by the focus on universalizing education and eradication of illiteracy, which often targets marginalized populations. Other categories like 'Children' or 'Women' are not explicitly mentioned, and 'Population in conflict' is not referenced.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 34. While strengthening coordination and collaboration among partners within the country itself and making optimal use of available international resources, Member States should also take into account the financial support opportunities presented by the World Conference on Education for All held in Thailand in March 1990. In doing so, they should further reinforce and develop bilateral, regional, and international cooperation, with the aim of adopting and implementing renewed policies and strategies for education for all, and actively participate in the regional programs and projects established by UNESCO to achieve this objective through collective effort. They should also provide necessary support to regional networks for research, innovation, and development working to improve the quality of education.

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes renewed policies and strategies for 'Education for All' (Policy theme). It highlights international collaboration and regional programs (International cooperation and partnerships). Mention of financial support opportunities and utilizing international resources ties to Financing and resource mobilization. Other categories like Governance and planning or Education in emergencies are not explicitly addressed.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes Member States' role in policy development and international collaboration, aligning with 'Government and policy makers'. UNESCO's involvement as an international organization is explicitly mentioned, supporting that category. 'Other' is included due to references to regional networks and collective effort, which don't fit neatly into existing categories.
low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph emphasizes international collaboration, policy development, and 'Education for All' strategies, aligning with 'General (academic)' as it focuses on broad educational policies and cooperation. 'Combined general and vocational' is excluded because there's no mention of vocational/technical training. 'Vocational/technical (TVET)' is not supported by the text, which prioritizes policy and international efforts over specific skill-based education.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 35. The action undertaken by UNESCO in favor of literacy and the universalization of primary education in the Least Developed Countries, notably through regional programs in Latin America and the Caribbean, Africa, Asia and the Pacific, and in Arab States, must be strengthened.

low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph focuses on universalizing primary education and literacy, which aligns with general academic education. Vocational/technical categories are excluded as the text does not mention TVET or skills training. Combined categories are not selected because the emphasis is solely on academic literacy and primary education, not a blend of general and vocational.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes universalization of primary education and strengthening regional programs, aligning with 'Access and equity' for inclusivity. 'International cooperation and partnerships' is supported by references to regional programs across multiple regions. Other categories like 'Governance and planning' or 'Financing and resource mobilization' are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph mentions 'Least Developed Countries' which aligns with 'Low-income and disadvantaged groups'. Regional programs in diverse regions imply 'Rural and remote learners' as these areas are often underserved. Other categories like 'Children' or 'Women' are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Ownership/Provision: The paragraph references UNESCO's action, which is a public entity, and mentions strengthening regional programs, indicating state-run initiatives. 'Public' is the most fitting category. 'Public-private partnership' and others are not explicitly supported by the text.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 36. Within the framework of conducting a study on the feasibility of a European cooperation programme as recommended by the UNESCO General Conference at its twenty-fifth session, it would be desirable to present a report on the issues of illiteracy in the Europe region. This report should be prepared in collaboration with other international institutions, networks, and non-governmental organizations, taking into account their activities.

low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph focuses on reporting illiteracy issues through a collaborative study, aligning with academic research (General (academic)). Vocational/technical categories are excluded as the text does not mention skill-based training. Combined general and vocational is not selected due to lack of explicit vocational components.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes collaboration with international institutions and NGOs (supporting 'International cooperation and partnerships') and addresses illiteracy, a key access and equity issue. 'Access and equity' is directly tied to literacy as a fundamental educational right. 'Policy theme' was excluded as the focus is on reporting and collaboration rather than overarching policy frameworks.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes collaboration with 'other international institutions, networks, and non-governmental organizations', directly aligning with 'International organizations and donors' and 'Civil society and NGOs'. 'Government and policy makers' is excluded as the focus is on collaboration rather than direct governmental involvement. 'Education system monitoring & evaluation' is not explicitly mentioned, so it is omitted.
low_level_thinking.Education system monitoring & evaluation: The paragraph mentions a study on illiteracy reporting, which aligns with 'Research and surveys' and 'Education statistics and indicators'. 'Stakeholder focus' is supported by collaboration with international institutions and NGOs. 'Policy theme' and 'Education system monitoring & evaluation' were considered but excluded due to limited direct mention of evaluative frameworks or policy implementation language.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: At a time when the International Literacy Year (ILY) has created the psychological conditions for action based on renewed mobilization, many developing countries are unable to make sufficient progress due to a lack of financial resources. The burden of external debt, the increase in interest payments for servicing this debt, and various other economic factors have hindered educational progress in many of these countries. The support they will need during the 1990s in the form of educational materials, staff training, exchanges of innovative experiences, and fund mobilization must be seriously considered by bilateral donors and other multilateral and international financial institutions. In this context, donor agencies and recipient countries together might consider redefining their priorities and, where possible, simplifying their technical cooperation procedures, as well as exploring the possibility of reallocating certain funds currently devoted by developing countries to debt servicing toward the financing of basic education.

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes financial constraints, debt servicing, and fund reallocation (Financing and resource mobilization). It mentions donor-recipient priorities and technical cooperation (International cooperation and partnerships). Governance is addressed through redefining priorities and simplifying procedures (Governance and planning).
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes donor agencies and international financial institutions as key stakeholders, aligning with 'International organizations and donors'. It also mentions developing countries and their governments needing to redefine priorities and simplify procedures, supporting 'Government and policy makers'. Other categories like 'Parents and communities' or 'Civil society and NGOs' are not explicitly referenced.
low_level_thinking.Infrastructure & resources: The paragraph explicitly mentions 'educational materials' as a needed support, directly matching 'Teaching and learning materials'. 'Other' is included due to references to 'fund mobilization' and 'reallocation of funds', which don't fit specific categories but relate to resource allocation. Categories like 'School buildings' or 'ICT' are not mentioned, so they are excluded.
low_level_thinking.Ownership/Provision: The paragraph emphasizes donor agencies (public) and multilateral institutions collaborating with developing countries, suggesting public involvement. It also mentions redefining priorities and simplifying procedures between donors and recipients, indicating a public-private partnership model. Private for-profit and community/faith-based are excluded as the focus is on institutional cooperation, not private profit motives or local community initiatives.
low_level_thinking.Education level: The paragraph emphasizes 'basic education' and mentions 'educational materials' and 'staff training', aligning with primary education (ISCED 1). 'Multiple levels' is included due to references to systemic reforms and the need for redefining priorities across different educational stages. Excluded categories like secondary or tertiary levels are not explicitly mentioned.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 38. As the institution invited to implement the International Literacy Year and as an international organization dedicated to education, UNESCO should endeavor to (a) support Member States requesting assistance in the development of their action plans for Education for All; (b) strengthen its operational capacities and the implementation of coordinated actions with other organizations (UNICEF, UNDP, World Bank, bilateral agencies, NGOs, etc.) in the field, in accordance with the World Declaration on Education for All and the Framework for Action to Meet Basic Learning Needs; (c) encourage larger-scale personnel training in the areas of planning and management, curricula and teaching, research and evaluation, and other related fields, in order to enhance the quality of literacy and basic education efforts in Member States through both formal and non-formal approaches; (d) take the initiative, at the request of the respective governments, in each country where widespread illiteracy persists, to implement appropriate programmes and projects; (e) organize the dissemination of information and expert exchanges as well as the sharing of ideas and materials within the framework of seminars and workshops bringing together various officials from Member States and through the establishment and strengthening of regional and international information exchange networks; (f) reinforce the capacities of its regional offices and international institutes, namely the International Bureau of Education (IBE), the International Institute for Educational Planning (IIEP), and the International Institute for Higher Education in Latin America and the Caribbean (IESALC), so as to be able to stimulate research, innovation, and evaluation programmes in Member States; (g) promote closer cooperation among Member States themselves through its regional innovation networks, regional offices, and international institutes such as the Asian Cultural Centre for UNESCO (ACCU), the Regional Centre for Adult Education and Functional Literacy in Latin America (CREFAL), and the Arab Organization for Adult Literacy and Education (ARLO); (h) encourage cooperation among non-governmental organizations working at various levels and seek their collaboration and participation in UNESCO’s activities in this field; (i) develop during the 1990s, in cooperation with Member States and the relevant United Nations demographic services, mechanisms to improve the reliability of literacy statistics and to monitor progress in different regions, as well as internationally, in order to periodically provide Member States and other entities with information on progress towards Education for All, the obstacles and blockages identified, where applicable, in specific regions or subregions, and the necessary adjustments to plans and programmes to achieve the goals set for the decade; (j) evaluate globally the impact of the International Literacy Year with a view to stimulating action throughout the remainder of the decade; (k) provide, upon request, Member States and interested non-governmental organizations with information and assistance to help them identify external sources of funding for their major programmes and projects to reduce illiteracy and promote basic education.

low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph emphasizes UNESCO's role in education planning, curriculum development, and research (general academic). It also mentions vocational training areas like personnel training in curricula and teaching, and collaboration with NGOs and bilateral agencies (combined general and vocational). 'Vocational/technical' was excluded as the focus is broader than TVET-specific skills.
low_level_thinking.Education system monitoring & evaluation: The paragraph emphasizes monitoring progress (education statistics), developing mechanisms for literacy data (EMIS), and evaluating global impact (institutional evaluation). 'Learning assessments' and 'research' are excluded as the focus is on data collection/monitoring, not assessments or surveys.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes UNESCO's role (international organization) collaborating with governments (policy makers) and NGOs (civil society) to implement Education for All. It highlights partnerships with UN agencies, bilateral agencies, NGOs, and regional bodies, aligning with stakeholder focus. 'Government and policy makers' are directly mentioned as key partners, while 'Civil society and NGOs' are explicitly referenced for collaboration. 'International organizations and donors' fits UNESCO's coordination with UN agencies and donor entities.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes UNESCO's role in coordinating with multiple stakeholders (International cooperation), planning and implementation of action plans (Governance and planning), promoting adult literacy (Lifelong learning and adult education), and securing funding mechanisms (Financing and resource mobilization). Categories like Quality and learning outcomes and Curriculum policies are less emphasized here.
low_level_thinking.Ownership/Provision: The paragraph emphasizes UNESCO's role as a public institution (Member States, regional offices) and its coordination with diverse stakeholders (UNICEF, NGOs, bilateral agencies) indicating PPP. 'Public' is directly supported by UNESCO's state-involved mandate, while 'PPP' reflects collaborative efforts with both public and non-profit entities. Private for-profit and community-based actors are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Learning modality: The paragraph explicitly mentions both formal and non-formal approaches in education (point c), and references lifelong learning through personnel training and capacity-building across various sectors. 'Lifelong learning' is directly implied by the emphasis on continuous training and monitoring over the decade. 'Formal education' is addressed through structured programs and curriculum development, while 'Non-formal education' is highlighted via adult literacy initiatives and regional networks. 'Informal learning' is not explicitly mentioned, so it is excluded.
low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph emphasizes UNESCO's role in developing curricula (Design and development), implementing literacy programs (Curriculum implementation), and evaluating progress through monitoring and data collection (Curriculum evaluation and reform). 'Content and learning areas' is excluded as the focus is on implementation rather than specific content. 'Competency-based' and 'learning outcomes' are not explicitly mentioned. 'Other curriculum-related' is avoided due to specificity.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes curriculum development, teaching methodologies, and training for educators (pedagogical foundations). It mentions evaluation of literacy programs and progress monitoring (assessment). Collaboration among institutions and continuous capacity-building aligns with professional learning communities. Technology-supported pedagogy and stakeholder focus are excluded as not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Infrastructure & resources: The paragraph emphasizes UNESCO's role in developing educational programs, sharing materials (teaching and learning materials), and coordinating with international bodies. It mentions information exchange networks and data monitoring, aligning with ICT and digital infrastructure. 'Infrastructure & resources' is directly referenced as a high-level label, encompassing UNESCO's capacity-building efforts.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes UNESCO's role in coordinating multi-stakeholder efforts (stakeholder focus), monitoring literacy progress (education system monitoring & evaluation), and promoting formal/non-formal learning modalities. 'Other cross-cutting theme' captures broader collaborative strategies. 'Policy theme' aligns with action plans and implementation frameworks. Excluded categories like 'inclusive education' or 'digital literacy' lack direct evidence.
low_level_thinking.Subject domain: The paragraph focuses on literacy initiatives (Literacy and language), UNESCO's role in education planning and collaboration (Social sciences and humanities), and broader educational frameworks (Other subject area). STEM, arts, and vocational skills are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Legal frameworks: The paragraph outlines UNESCO's operational responsibilities (policy implementation) and collaborative actions with stakeholders (rights/obligations). Monitoring literacy progress and evaluating impacts align with education system monitoring. 'Legal frameworks' is broader than the specific implementation and evaluation focus here.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 39. The International Bureau of Education should, in collaboration and coordination with other UNESCO units: (a) monitor the present Conference and advise Member States requesting assistance on ways to implement this Recommendation in light of experiences gained by other countries, both developing and industrialized; (b) further strengthen its activities related to documentation and the worldwide dissemination of publications on literacy and education for all; (c) conduct comparative research and monitor progress made by different Member States and regions to ensure "Education for All" by the year 2000, utilizing, among other resources, the documents of the World Conference on Education for All of which it is the custodian, as well as information gathered through national reports; (d) assist developing countries by organizing training programs for the establishment of regional and national networks for documentation exchange; (e) publish additional booklets in the series "Literacy? Let’s Talk About It!" in various languages and widely distribute them throughout the 1990s;

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes collaboration with UNESCO units (international cooperation), monitoring progress towards Education for All (monitoring and evaluation), and coordinating implementation strategies (governance and planning). Categories like 'Access and equity' and 'Lifelong learning' are less directly supported, while 'Policy theme' is covered through governance functions.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes the International Bureau of Education's role in monitoring progress and providing guidance to Member States, aligning with 'Government and policy makers' as it involves advising states on implementation. 'International organizations and donors' is relevant as the Bureau is part of UNESCO, an international organization. Other categories like 'Teachers and educators' or 'Learners and students' are not directly mentioned, and 'Civil society and NGOs' are not referenced in the text.

TITLE: LA LUTTE CONTRE L'ANALPHABETISME : POLITIQUES, STRATEGIES ET PROGRAMMES OPERATIONNELS POUR LES ANNEES 90 PREAMBULE
paragraph: 40. The Conference recommends that a first report on the implementation of the present Recommendation be presented at the 43rd session of the IBE.

low_level_thinking.Education system monitoring & evaluation: The paragraph mentions a 'report on the implementation' which aligns with 'Education statistics and indicators' as it involves data collection and reporting. 'Institutional evaluation and inspection' is relevant due to the mention of monitoring and evaluation in the context of policy implementation. Other categories like 'Learning assessments' or 'EMIS' are not directly supported by the text.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph mentions the presentation of a report on implementation, which relates to governance and planning as it involves policy monitoring and reporting mechanisms. Accountability and transparency are also relevant as the report serves to ensure compliance and openness in policy execution. Other categories like financing or curriculum are not directly addressed in the text.
low_level_thinking.Legal frameworks: The paragraph mentions the presentation of a report on the implementation of the Recommendation, which directly relates to policy implementation and enforcement. Other categories like international conventions and treaties are not explicitly referenced, and the focus is on monitoring and reporting rather than legal disputes or rights frameworks.

TITLE: LA CONTRIBUTION DE L'’EDUCATION AU DEVELOPPEMENT CULTUREL
paragraph: The International Conference on Education (ICE), convened by the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization, held in Geneva for its 43rd session, from September 14 to 19, 1992,

low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph references the International Conference on Education (ICE) and its focus on educational policy, which aligns with 'General (academic)' as it pertains to broader educational themes. 'Vocational/technical (TVET)' and 'Combined general and vocational' are excluded because the text does not mention specific vocational training or combined educational approaches.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph only mentions the convening of the International Conference on Education (ICE) by UNESCO without specifying any particular policy themes or outcomes. Other categories require explicit mention of policies related to access, quality, governance, etc., which are absent here. 'Other policy theme' is the only category that accommodates the general reference to an educational conference without specific policy details.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph mentions the International Conference on Education (ICE) convened by UNESCO, which is an international organization. No other stakeholder groups are explicitly referenced. Categories like 'Government and policy makers' and 'Civil society and NGOs' are not directly mentioned.

TITLE: LA CONTRIBUTION DE L'’EDUCATION AU DEVELOPPEMENT CULTUREL
paragraph: Convinced of the primacy of the human being, not only as a factor but also as the ultimate goal of all development, and consequently of the necessity to promote their fulfillment in all its dimensions through, in particular, broader access to culture and education,

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes broader access to education (Access and equity), fulfillment through education (Quality and learning outcomes), and the human dimension implying inclusion (Inclusion and diversity). TITLE: LA CONTRIBUTION DE L'’EDUCATION AU DEVELOPPEMENT CULTUREL
