TITLE: PROPOSITIONS D'ACTION PROPOSITIONS D'ACTION ISSUES DE LA 46e SESSION DE LA CIE [ADDED]
paragraph: Reform processes should be pursued or undertaken in the following areas: > Content • By adapting curricula and updating content to take into account: the economic and social changes brought about, in particular, by globalization, migration, and cultural diversity; the ethical dimension of scientific and technological developments; the growing importance of communication, expression, the ability to listen and engage in dialogue, first in the mother tongue, then in the country’s official language, as well as in one or more foreign languages; the positive contribution that the integration of technologies can bring to learning processes. • By developing not only disciplinary but also interdisciplinary approaches and skills. • By encouraging and supporting innovations. • By seeking, in curriculum development, to ensure their relevance at local, national, and international levels. > Methods • By prioritizing active learning methods and teamwork. • By fostering a comprehensive and balanced development of the personality, preparing the individual for the practice of active citizenship open to the world. > Teachers • By facilitating genuine teacher participation in decision-making within the institution, through training and other means. • By improving teacher training so that they can develop in students behaviors oriented toward values of solidarity and tolerance, preparing them to prevent and peacefully resolve conflicts as well as to respect cultural diversity. By evolving the teacher-student relationship in light of societal changes. • By enhancing the use of information and communication technologies in teacher training and classroom practice. > Daily life in educational institutions • By creating a climate of tolerance and respect at school that fosters the development of a democratic culture. • By offering a mode of school operation that encourages student participation in decision-making. • By proposing a shared definition of projects and learning activities. > Educational research • By developing research that clarifies the concept of Learning to Live Together and its impact on educational policies and practices. • By promoting research on the evolution of content and methodologies related to Learning to Live Together. • By stimulating the conduct of comparative studies to be implemented within subregional, regional, and trans-regional frameworks.

low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph discusses curriculum design and development (e.g., adapting curricula, interdisciplinary approaches), content and learning areas (e.g., ethical dimension, communication skills), implementation (e.g., teacher participation, technology use), and competency-based outcomes (e.g., values of solidarity, conflict resolution). 'Curriculum evaluation and reform' is not explicitly mentioned, and 'Other curriculum-related issue' is too broad.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes curriculum reform (pedagogical foundations), active learning methods (teaching approaches), ICT integration (technology-supported pedagogy), and teacher training/decision-making (professional learning communities). Assessment is not explicitly mentioned, and 'other pedagogy' is excluded due to lack of distinct themes.
low_level_thinking.Teachers: The paragraph emphasizes improving teacher training (Professional development) and evolving teacher-student relationships (Teacher other). Other categories like recruitment or working conditions are not explicitly mentioned. Legal status and technology use are touched on but not sufficient for separate labels.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph focuses on curriculum reform, pedagogical methods, teacher training, and inclusive practices, aligning with 'Curriculum and pedagogy policies' and 'Teachers and workforce policies'. Inclusion of diverse perspectives and cultural respect supports 'Inclusion and diversity'. Cross-regional research and comparative studies indicate 'International cooperation and partnerships'. Other categories like governance or financing are not explicitly addressed.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes teacher participation, training, and evolving relationships (Teachers and educators). Learners are addressed through active learning methods, citizenship preparation, and inclusive environments (Learners and students). Government and policy makers are implied through curriculum development and institutional reforms (Government and policy makers). Civil society/parents/other stakeholders are not explicitly mentioned.

TITLE: PROPOSITIONS D'ACTION PROPOSITIONS D'ACTION ISSUES DE LA 46e SESSION DE LA CIE [ADDED]
paragraph: The development of international cooperation activities in support of improving the quality of education for all to Learn to Live Together should be structured around six main axes: > strengthening the International Bureau of Education as an observatory of trends as well as its role in developing easily accessible databases and information systems; > collecting research findings in education on the evolution of content, promoting comparative studies at subregional and regional levels, and disseminating them at the international level; > creating cooperation networks at international, regional, and subregional levels to facilitate the exchange of experiences and the implementation of joint projects in order to strengthen endogenous capacities; > training policymakers in educational systems in political dialogue to promote the definition of convergent objectives, the search for consensus, and the mobilization of stakeholders; > renewing the modalities of technical assistance provided by bilateral or multilateral cooperation agencies to better take into account not only the North-South dimension but also South-South cooperation; > strengthening partnerships between UNESCO and other relevant intergovernmental organizations.

'Citizenship and civic education' is excluded as the focus is broader than national civic education. 'Digital literacy' and 'financial literacy' are not mentioned. 'Sustainable development' is not explicitly addressed.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes international cooperation through networks and partnerships (International cooperation and partnerships). Governance is addressed via training policymakers and institutional reforms (Governance and planning). Quality is linked to 'Learning to Live Together' and comparative studies (Quality and learning outcomes). Excluded categories like curriculum or financing are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes training policymakers (Government and policy makers) and partnerships with UNESCO and other intergovernmental organizations (International organizations and donors). TITLE: PRIORITES D'ACTION PROPOSITIONS DE PRIORITÉS POUR L'ACTION EN VUE D'AMÉLIORER LA QUALITÉ DE L'ÉDUCATION DE TOUS LES JEUNES [ADDED]
paragraph: Gathered in Geneva from 8 to 11 September 2004 for the 47th session of the UNESCO International Conference on Education, we, Ministers, heads of delegation and delegates from 137 Member States, engaged in rich, frank, and open discussions on the theme: "Quality Education for All Young People: Challenges, Trends and Priorities," alongside representatives from 14 intergovernmental organizations and 21 NGOs and foundations. The young people present throughout the Conference also expressed their desire to benefit from education that meets their expectations and conveyed all the hopes they place in our actions.

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes 'Quality Education for All' directly linking to 'Quality and learning outcomes'. Youth inclusion and their expressed desires align with 'Inclusion and diversity'. Multiple stakeholders (intergovernmental orgs, NGOs) and global participation support 'International cooperation and partnerships'.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph explicitly mentions Ministers, heads of delegation, and delegates (government/policy makers). Learners and students are directly referenced through the mention of young people expressing their desires. Civil society and NGOs are included due to the presence of 21 NGOs and foundations. Government and policy makers are explicitly named, while international organizations are mentioned but not as stakeholders in the context of the debate.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph mentions youth expectations and their hopes for education, aligning with 'Life skills and 21st century competencies' as it relates to preparing young people for future challenges. 'Inclusive education and special needs' is implied by the presence of diverse stakeholders and the focus on 'all young people'. 'Other cross-cutting theme' is included due to the broad thematic nature of the conference and the mention of multiple stakeholder groups beyond specific categories.

TITLE: PRIORITES D'ACTION PROPOSITIONS DE PRIORITÉS POUR L'ACTION EN VUE D'AMÉLIORER LA QUALITÉ DE L'ÉDUCATION DE TOUS LES JEUNES [ADDED]
paragraph: This theme allowed us to express our concerns, share our difficulties, jointly seek potential solutions, affirm our hopes, and strengthen international dialogue on educational policies. Our work was central to the issue of Education for All (EFA) and the commitments made at Jomtien and Dakar, as well as, fundamentally, to the Millennium Declaration, the United Nations Decade of Education for Sustainable Development, and the Decade of Literacy. It is increasingly clear that basic education cannot be limited, regardless of the region of the world, to primary schooling alone.

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes global educational commitments (International cooperation and partnerships), sustainable development (Sustainable development and environment), lifelong learning beyond primary schooling (Lifelong learning and adult education), and quality education as foundational to EFA and Millennium Declaration (Quality and learning outcomes). 'Access and equity' is implied but not explicitly stated, while 'Education in emergencies' and 'Health' are absent.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes global educational commitments (EFA, Jomtien, Dakar, Millennium Declaration) and the importance of basic education beyond primary schooling, which aligns with 'Other cross-cutting theme' as it captures overarching priorities. Other categories like 'Inclusive education' or 'Sustainable development' are not explicitly mentioned, and 'Policy theme' is subsumed under the broader cross-cutting focus.
low_level_thinking.Education level: The paragraph explicitly mentions 'primary schooling' and emphasizes that 'basic education cannot be limited to primary schooling alone,' indicating a focus on primary education. 'Multiple levels' is included because the text references broader educational commitments and frameworks that span beyond a single level, such as the Millennium Declaration and the Decade of Education for Sustainable Development.
low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph emphasizes global educational commitments and basic education beyond primary schooling, aligning with 'General (academic)' as it reflects broader educational goals. 'Combined general and vocational' is excluded due to no mention of vocational aspects. 'Vocational/technical (TVET)' is not supported by the text focusing on general education frameworks.

TITLE: PRIORITES D'ACTION PROPOSITIONS DE PRIORITÉS POUR L'ACTION EN VUE D'AMÉLIORER LA QUALITÉ DE L'ÉDUCATION DE TOUS LES JEUNES [ADDED]
paragraph: 1. Promoting quality education and training for all young people aged 12 to 18/20 is a commitment to a better future and an antidote to social exclusion at the local, national, and global levels. As both a fundamental right and a public good, this education must meet the needs of all young people, in order to ensure their personal development, their integration into the workforce, as well as their ability to participate in an active and responsible civic life.

TITLE: PRIORITES D'ACTION PROPOSITIONS DE PRIORITÉS POUR L'ACTION EN VUE D'AMÉLIORER LA QUALITÉ DE L'ÉDUCATION DE TOUS LES JEUNES [ADDED]
paragraph: 2. This Conference has enabled us, in particular, to reach international agreement on several fundamental elements for the construction and strengthening of our educational policies. Among these are the need to: • recognize that, although all countries, both in the North and the South, face difficulties, the situation of youth education is particularly dire in many developing countries, whose educational systems appear inadequately adapted both to the needs of young people and those of societies; • assist young people in confronting an increasingly challenging world, characterized in particular by the challenges of humanizing globalization, by the significant role of information and knowledge as factors of production and development, by the increase in population movements, by the fragilization and marginalization of numerous social groups, as well as by the worsening inequalities and poverty within countries and globally; • continue, in a more practical and effective manner, actions aimed at improving access to and retention of girls in education, gender equality, and equity; • ensure that all young people acquire skills for personal autonomy and citizenship, for integration into the world of work, and for social life, with a view to respecting their identity and being open to the world and to social and cultural diversity; • strengthen, through education for active and responsible citizenship, the will to live together and to build peace in a world marked by inter- and intra-state conflicts and the emergence of all forms of violence and warfare; • reaffirm the irreplaceable role of teachers and trainers, whose professionalism must be enhanced, both through the elevation of their status and through their initial and ongoing training, so that they are able to meet the new demands that young people and societies place upon them.

low_level_thinking.Education orientation: The paragraph emphasizes both academic skills for citizenship and personal autonomy (General) and vocational skills for work integration (Combined). It explicitly mentions 'skills for personal autonomy and citizenship' and 'integration into the world of work', supporting both categories. 'Vocational/technical' is excluded as the focus is broader than just TVET.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes education for active citizenship and peace (Citizenship and civic education, Global citizenship and peace education). It addresses gender equality and equity (Human rights and equality) and mentions skills for autonomy, citizenship, and social life (Life skills and 21st century competencies). Digital/ICT literacy and other categories are not explicitly mentioned.
low_level_thinking.Teachers: The paragraph explicitly mentions enhancing teachers' professionalism through initial and ongoing training, supporting 'Professional development'. It also emphasizes the irreplaceable role of teachers and their need to meet societal demands, aligning with 'Teacher other'. Categories like recruitment, working conditions, or legal status are not directly addressed.
low_level_thinking.Learner population: The paragraph explicitly mentions 'youth education' and challenges faced by 'girls in education', directly linking to 'Youth and adolescents' and 'Women and girls'. 'Low-income and disadvantaged groups' is supported by references to 'worsening inequalities and poverty'. Other categories like 'Indigenous or minority groups' and 'migrants' are not explicitly mentioned, though 'social groups' is referenced but not specific enough to justify broader categories.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes access to education for girls (Access and equity), skills development for citizenship and work (Quality and learning outcomes), teacher professionalism (Teachers and workforce policies), diversity and inclusion (Inclusion and diversity), and global collaboration (International cooperation and partnerships). 'Governance' and 'Financing' are not explicitly mentioned, while 'Lifelong learning' and 'Sustainable development' are not directly addressed.

TITLE: PRIORITES D'ACTION PROPOSITIONS DE PRIORITÉS POUR L'ACTION EN VUE D'AMÉLIORER LA QUALITÉ DE L'ÉDUCATION DE TOUS LES JEUNES [ADDED]
paragraph: Our educational systems, often inherited from the past, no longer allow, in many countries, to meet all the challenges they face. New, broader, and more diverse paths must be found, and it is clear that improving the quality of education for young people cannot be the responsibility solely of public authorities, teachers, and formal educational systems. Education is the concern of all.

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes improving education quality for young people (Quality and learning outcomes). It mentions systemic challenges requiring collaboration beyond public authorities (Governance and planning). The call for diverse paths and education as a shared concern implies inclusion and diversity.

'Other cross-cutting theme' is selected due to the focus on systemic challenges and collaborative responsibility, which doesn't fit neatly into other categories. Categories like 'Inclusive education and special needs' and 'Digital and ICT literacy' are excluded as the paragraph does not specifically mention inclusivity or digital skills.
low_level_thinking.Ownership/Provision: The paragraph mentions 'public authorities' and 'formal educational systems' indicating public involvement. It implies multiple stakeholders (public, private, community) as education is described as 'the concern of all'. Private entities and community-based initiatives are suggested by the need for 'new, broader, and more diverse paths'. 'Public-private partnership' is not explicitly mentioned but implied through the collective responsibility.

TITLE: PRIORITES D'ACTION PROPOSITIONS DE PRIORITÉS POUR L'ACTION EN VUE D'AMÉLIORER LA QUALITÉ DE L'ÉDUCATION DE TOUS LES JEUNES [ADDED]
paragraph: 4. At the conclusion of this Conference, we reaffirm the paramount importance of education in our national development policies. Consequently, we call for the mobilization of all partners to be able to meet the immense challenges of youth education for a human-faced globalization and sustainable development of our societies and the entire planet. Only new alliances and effective partnerships will make it possible to find diverse and appropriate responses to the current and future needs of young people and to improve the quality of their education. These alliances must be built and strengthened within the States themselves, among governments, civil society, the economic sector, the media, and the youth themselves. They must also be realized and reinforced at regional and international levels, among all development partners so that the commitments made by the international community to support the improvement of education quality can be fulfilled.

'Learners and students' and 'Parents and communities' are not directly mentioned, so they are excluded.

We also express the hope that the Conclusions and Priority Proposals for Action, which we adopted unanimously on September 11, 2004, may genuinely become a source of inspiration for our educational policies and serve to bring about a strong and lasting improvement in the quality of education for all young people worldwide, for peace, justice, and sustainable development.

paragraph: 6. Impressive figures: Currently, half of the world’s population is under the age of 25. The world is home to the largest youth generation it has ever known. More than one billion people are between the ages of 12 and 20. By 2020, 87% of young people worldwide will live in developing countries, where they are already the majority today. Meanwhile, developed countries will face the demographic challenge of an increasingly aging population.

TITLE: PRIORITES D'ACTION PROPOSITIONS DE PRIORITÉS POUR L'ACTION EN VUE D'AMÉLIORER LA QUALITÉ DE L'ÉDUCATION DE TOUS LES JEUNES [ADDED]
paragraph: 7. Broader access and opportunities, but still significant challenges: The number of young people completing primary education is continuously increasing. The number of enrollments in secondary education worldwide has multiplied tenfold over the past 50 years. While global secondary school enrollment rates have risen from 56% to 77.5% during the last decade, this average conceals the reality of millions of young people, particularly in the Global South, who, despite finishing primary school, are still deprived of the opportunity to earn a living or continue their education. Moreover, a large number of them have not even completed primary education. Girls are especially affected.

Consequently, the issues related to the quality of education for this age group have now become central to national and international priorities.

paragraph: 10. Life skills: Currently, education systems, including technical and vocational education and training, do not provide young people with the opportunity to acquire and strengthen the complex skills necessary for life. Many of these skills are essential to overcoming contemporary challenges such as HIV/AIDS and other pandemics, transitioning from education to the labor market, as well as social and political disengagement. 'Vocational/technical (TVET)' is excluded as the focus is on language and culture, not technical skills. 'Combined general and vocational' is rejected since there's no mention of vocational aspects.

'Other cross-cutting theme' is included due to the unique focus on mother tongue promotion, which doesn't fit neatly into existing categories. Categories like 'Human rights and equality' and 'Global citizenship' are excluded as the text lacks explicit references to rights frameworks or global citizenship concepts.
low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph explicitly mentions linguistic and cultural diversity, aligning with 'Inclusion and diversity'. TITLE: RECOMMANDATIONS CONCERNANT L'EDUCATION POUR L'INCLUSION [ADDED]
paragraph: 9. Encourage education stakeholders to design genuine curricular frameworks from early childhood, while adopting a flexible approach that takes into account local needs and situations and diversifies pedagogical practices.

low_level_thinking.Curriculum: The paragraph emphasizes designing curricular frameworks (Design and development), adopting flexible approaches with diversified pedagogical practices (Curriculum implementation), and implies learning outcomes through 'genuine curricular frameworks' and 'local needs' aligning with competency-based outcomes. 'Competency-based and learning outcomes' is included as the focus on effective, adaptable frameworks suggests outcome-oriented design. 'Learning modality' is excluded as the flexible approach refers to pedagogical diversity, not specific modalities.
low_level_thinking.Pedagogy & assessment: The paragraph emphasizes designing curricular frameworks and adopting flexible, diversified pedagogical practices, aligning with 'Pedagogical foundations and theories' and 'Teaching methods and approaches'. 'Assessment and feedback' is excluded as the paragraph focuses on curriculum and teaching practices, not evaluation. 'Learning modality' is debated but not explicitly mentioned, so it's omitted.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes collaboration with stakeholders (teachers, parents, civil society) to design curricula and adapt pedagogy. 'Teachers and educators' are directly mentioned as key actors. 'Parents and communities' and 'Civil society and NGOs' are implied through references to local needs and diversified practices. 'Government and policy makers' are not explicitly mentioned, and other categories like private sector or international organizations are not referenced.
low_level_thinking.Cross-cutting themes & skills: The paragraph emphasizes flexible curricular frameworks and diversified pedagogical practices, aligning with 'Life skills and 21st century competencies' through adaptive learning approaches. 'Inclusive education and special needs' is supported by the mention of local needs and diverse practices. 'Other cross-cutting theme' covers the flexible approach and stakeholder collaboration not fully captured by other categories.

Other categories like 'Children' or 'Youth' are not specified, and 'ICT' is discussed as a tool, not a learner group.

TITLE: RECOMMANDATIONS CONCERNANT L'EDUCATION POUR L'INCLUSION [ADDED]
paragraph: 22. Recognize UNESCO's leadership role in the field of education for inclusion through the following actions: • Encourage exchanges and dissemination of good practices; • Provide countries, upon request, with advice on how they can develop and implement policies for education for inclusion; • Promote South-South and North-South-South cooperation to advance education for inclusion; • Support efforts to increase resources allocated to education at both national and international levels; • Make special efforts to assist the Least Developed Countries and countries affected by conflicts in the implementation of these recommendations.

low_level_thinking.Policy theme: The paragraph emphasizes education for inclusion, directly linking to 'Inclusion and diversity'. It mentions UNESCO's leadership and cooperation mechanisms like South-South partnerships, supporting 'International cooperation and partnerships'. Resource allocation and support for LDCs and conflict-affected countries align with 'Financing and resource mobilization'. 'Access and equity' is related but not explicitly mentioned. 'Education in emergencies and crises' is partially relevant but not directly stated.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes UNESCO's leadership (international organizations) and policy development (government/policymakers). It mentions supporting countries (governments) and special efforts for vulnerable groups (parents/communities). Civil society/NGOs and private sector are not explicitly referenced, while learners are not directly mentioned as stakeholders in the actions listed.
